coredata.dc:title: Honeycomb waste-derived carbon dots as a sensitive sensing probe for detection of capecitabine chemo drug
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

coredata.dc:creator: Khandagale, Pradnya
Chougale, Prathamesh
Mujawar, Nafeesa
Momin, Muskan
Dhabbe, Rohant
Kakade, Rajratna
Nipane, Sandip
Sabale, Sandip
Kim, Deok-kee
coredata.dc:description: Anticancer medication provoked concerns owing to its adverse health effects from overdose and henceforth, its sensitive monitoring is crucial. Carbon dots (CDs), as a pioneering carbon nanomaterial, have particles lower than 10 nm. CDs have an extensive multitude of applications based on their luminous qualities. The present era is focused on turning waste into economically viable products. The current research demonstrates a feasible method for preparing green fluorescent CDs from honeycomb waste (HCCDs). The HCCDs display excitation-dependent emission properties, exhibiting a blue shift with a change in excitation wavelength, and acquiring good stability with a zeta potential of −14.8 mV. Nevertheless, the particle size ranges between 2–5 nm. It is noteworthy that the fluorescence intensity of HCCDs was remarkably enhanced by the addition of increasing concentrations of capecitabine due to complex formation. Additionally, the sensor shows a determined detection limit of 1.04 μmolL−1 without interference from ions. This demonstrates exclusive selectivity and sensitivity which paves a new way for the determination of the capecitabine drug.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Honeycomb
Capecitabine
Fluorescence enhancement
Sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots from honeycomb (HCCDs)
XRD and HR-TEM analysis of HCCDs
Optimization of experimental conditions for detection of capecitabine
Selective fluorescent probing of capecitabine and interference study
HCCD as a sensor for capecitabine and validity of the method
Insight in to the fluorescence enhancement mechanism and testing of real sample

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematics illustrating the synthesis of HCCD.
(a) FT-IR (b) XPS Survey (c) C1s, and (d) O1s spectrum of HCCDs.
Zeta Potential distribution graph of HCCDs.
(a) and (c) HR-TEM images (b) SAED pattern of HCCDs and (d) HCCDs + cap complex.
(a) PL intensity at different excitation wavelengths (b) UV absorbance graph (c) pH-dependent graph (d) Time-dependent graph (e) Temperature-dependent graph of HCCDs.
(a) Selectivity bar graph of various amino acids and capecitabine (b) PL spectrum of HCCDs with different ions, and (c) Interference bar graph.
(a) PL spectra of HCCDs with various concentrations of capecitabine and (b) with error (c) Calibration graph.
Schematic representation of HCCDs-cap PL enhancement mechanism.
Comparisons of different methods used for analysis of Capecitabine.
Real sample analysis of pharmaceutical sample and recovery.

head.ce:title: Honeycomb waste-derived carbon dots as a sensitive sensing probe for detection of capecitabine chemo drug

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Jaysingpur College, Jaysingpur 416101, MS, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Jaysingpur College, Jaysingpur 416101, MS, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Smt. Kasturbai Walchand College of Arts and Science, Sangli 416416, MS, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Smt. Kasturbai Walchand College of Arts and Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Smt. Kasturbai Walchand College, Sangli 416416, MS, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Botany, Padmbhushan Dr. Vasantraodada Patil Mahavidyalaya, Tasgaon 416312, MS, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Botany, Padmbhushan Dr. Vasantraodada Patil Mahavidyalaya, Tasgaon 416312, MS, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Semiconductor Systems Engineering and Convergence Engineering for Intelligent Drone, Sejong University, Seoul 05006, South Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Semiconductor Systems Engineering and Convergence Engineering for Intelligent Drone

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Semiconductor Systems Engineering and Convergence Engineering for Intelligent Drone, Sejong University, Seoul 05006, South Korea

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Department of Chemistry, Jaysingpur College, Jaysingpur 416101, MS, India (S. Sabale).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Anticancer medication provoked concerns owing to its adverse health effects from overdose and henceforth, its sensitive monitoring is crucial. Carbon dots (CDs), as a pioneering carbon nanomaterial, have particles lower than 10 nm. CDs have an extensive multitude of applications based on their luminous qualities. The present era is focused on turning waste into economically viable products. The current research demonstrates a feasible method for preparing green fluorescent CDs from honeycomb waste (HCCDs). The HCCDs display excitation-dependent emission properties, exhibiting a blue shift with a change in excitation wavelength, and acquiring good stability with a zeta potential of −14.8 mV. Nevertheless, the particle size ranges between 2–5 nm. It is noteworthy that the fluorescence intensity of HCCDs was remarkably enhanced by the addition of increasing concentrations of capecitabine due to complex formation. Additionally, the sensor shows a determined detection limit of 1.04 μmolL−1 without interference from ions. This demonstrates exclusive selectivity and sensitivity which paves a new way for the determination of the capecitabine drug.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) were known as “fluorescent nanoparticles” and “carbogenic dots” in early literature [1]. CDs are biocompatible, environmentally benign, and low toxic materials. Additionally, considering their excellent optical properties, including light stability, anti-photobleaching, and broad excitation wavelength, they are utilized as fluorescent material [2]. The surface functionalities have a significant impact on fluorescence intensity. The interaction of these functional moieties and other chemicals can either enhance or quench fluorescence [3]. An array of carbon-rich raw materials, including plant waste and most food by-products, has been used for the preparation of CDs. Furthermore, food waste from fruits, vegetables, livestock, and other sources is recycled. Fluorescent CDs are therefore utilized in a wide spectrum ofapplications that are deemed to be environmentally benign and bio-convenient [4]. CDs have a variety of applications [5], including probes for food safety [6]. Identify a variety of pollutants, including pesticides [7], heavy metals [8], veterinary medicines, microorganisms, illegal additives, and fluorescence [9,10].

Pharmaceuticals are crucial to healthcare because they may treat a wide range of ailments, but they must be used cautiously to prevent side effects. The development of innovative and highly selective analytical techniques is essential for quicker, reliable method that conserve assets and reduce use of toxic or hazardous solvents as pharmaceutical research progresses.

Further, biomass produced leftover waste that is poisonous, which goes against the principles of reusing waste materials. Therefore, in the current era of nanoscale materials, the demand for clean materials in the synthesis of CDs has become essential. Adopting the use of clean materials in CDs synthesis is beneficial for a variety of portable applications, including medicine delivery, water purification, bioimaging, and many more [11]. Using waste materials or biomass for processes that further release toxins and have an impact on the environment and human health is unethical. The obvious scientific flaw of using biomass in CDs synthesis is that it lacks the structural homogeneity and fundamental purity, time of synthesis methods. etc. For this reason, using clean products is essential [12].

Honeycombs are discarded after extraction of honey. Ultimately these are falling down and turning into trash. Although it contains a large amount of valuable ingredients. The primary component of honeycomb waste is beeswax. Beeswax, a complex mixture of hydrocarbons and lipids, makes up the majority of the material that makes up honeycomb. The main components of beeswax are long-chain alcohols, fatty acids, and esters. The majority of compounds present are long-chain alkanes, acids, esters, polyesters, and hydroxy esters [13]. Hydrocarbons are main components found in beeswax [14]. These compounds can act as fluorophores, which are pivotal to the fluorescence.

Capecitabine is a chemotherapeutic drug used to treat breast, colon, and rectal cancers. Like many other chemotherapy drugs, capecitabine might have side effects. Common side effects may include diarrhoea, nausea and vomiting, hair loss, fatigue, change in taste, loss of appetite, hand and foot swelling, and pain etc. [15]. Nevertheless, multiple techniques have been employed, such as, RP-HPLC and UV spectroscopy [16], UHPLC-MS/MS [17], and spectrofluorometry [18]. Especially fluorescence is the most efficient and economical technique. Consequently, developing an innovative approach for sensitive and selective drug detection through the synthesis of green carbon dots could potentially be worthwhile.

In the present research work, honeycomb after the extraction of honey is used as the carbon source. The current study explores the sensing study of capecitabine by applying HCCDs as a promising sensing probe with a low detection limit, great stability, good biocompatibility, and unique selectivity. Based on the existing literature, this may be the first attempt to demonstrate CDs as a sensing probe for the detection of the capecitabine drug.

This advancement not only enhances the accuracy of capecitabine measurement but also opens up possibilities for real-time monitoring in clinical settings. Furthermore, the ability to detect such low concentrations with minimal interference could significantly improve the outcome by this method.

The honeycomb is collected from the local garden of Sangli city, Maharashtra. The chemicals, sulfuric acid, nitric acid, ammonia, sodium hydroxide was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. The API grade capecitabine, amino acids (Uracil, L-cystine, L-arginine, histidine, L-proline, thymine) were made available from HIMEDIA, India. All chemicals were of AR Grade and were used as received. Distilled water was used throughout the experiment.

A (UV 3000+) UV–vis absorption spectrometer was used to record the absorption spectra. The fluorescence measurement is carried out by a spectrofluorometer (Jasco spectrofluorometer, Japan, F-8200). The HR-TEM and SAED patterns are from the JEM-2100 plus model. The FT-IR 4600, Jasco, Japan equipment was used to gather the infrared data. A Bruker D8/Advance powder X-ray diffractometer provided the X-ray diffraction measurement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots from honeycomb (HCCDs)
ce:section.ce:para: An easy chemical technique was used for the preparation of CDs from honeycomb [19]. The honeycombs are thoroughly cleaned in water and afterward allowed to dry in sunlight for a day. The dried honeycomb was cut into small pieces, weighed precisely at 1 g, and afterwards dispersed in 100 mL of 10 % sodium hydroxide. Next, the mixture is then heated on a magnetic stirrer, by maintaining the temperature between 60 and 70℃, until the solution turns brown. The brown color symbolizes the production of carbon dots (Fig. 1 ). This solution is then filtered through a 22 µm filter paper followed by dialysis through a dialysis membrane 110 (HiMedia). The as-prepared HCCDs are kept in the refrigerator for further use.

The potential of synthesized HCCDs as a sensing probe was verified using spectrofluorometry (PL). The effects of various parameters such as pH, time, and temperature on sensing performance were optimized by performing triplicate measurements. Additionally, interference and selectivity were also examined using triplicate measurements. Based on these triplicate measurements the error bars of the resulting outputs were derived.

Using quinine sulfate solution in 0.1 M H2SO4 as the standard reference solution, the quantum yield (QY) was calculated using equation 1 as follows [20]
(1)
∅ s
=
∅ R
×
I S
I R
×
A R
A S
×
η
S
2
η
R
2
where ∅S represents QY of HCCDs; ∅R represents QY of reference solution (0.54); IS and IR represents integrated fluorescent emission intensity of the sample and reference, respectively; AS and AR represent absorbance of sample and reference, respectively; ηS and ηR represents refractive indices of the HCCDs and standard reference solvents, respectively, which is 1.33.

Moreover, the experimentally optimized conditions were explored for the real sample analysis. Further, the standard addition method was used to evaluate the feasibility of the HCCDs in real sample detection of the capecitabine drug in tablet Capsy (500 mg). Initially, the stock solution of 0.01 M Capsy tablet was prepared. Using this stock solution, solutions of varying concentrations including 5, 25 and 50 µmolL−1 were prepared and fluorescence intensity was measured.

The functional groups present in HCCDs were assessed using FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 2 a). The IR spectrum of HCCDs showed three prominent peaks at 3377 cm−1, 1776 cm−1, and 1420 cm1, corresponding to –OH, C = O, and –OH bending vibration, which could be attributed to the functional groups present in the honeycomb. Whereas the peaks at 697 and 877 cm−1 are accounted to C–H bending, C–H bending of monosubstituted aromatic compounds, respectively [21].

To study the elemental composition, XPS characterization was carried out. The survey spectrum (Fig. 2b) reveals carbon and oxygen were major constituent atoms. In the deconvoluted C1s (Fig. 2c) spectrum, four major peaks were observed with binding energies of 284.8, 286.0, 287.17, and 290.2 eV, which are attributed to C = C/C–C, C-O, C = O, and π-π* transitions, respectively. Similarly, the high-resolution spectrum of O1s (Fig. 2d) shows two peaks with binding energies of 531.2 and 532.6 eV attributed to C-O and C = O, respectively [22–24].

To investigate the stability and the charge over the surface of as-synthesized HCCDs, zeta potential analysis was performed (Fig. 3 ). The zeta potential analysis shows a mean value of −14.8 mV, which indicates the negative charge moieties, such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, etc., over the surface of HCCDs [25]. The obtained zeta potential value confirms the higher stability of prepared HCCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: XRD and HR-TEM analysis of HCCDs
ce:section.ce:para: By using the XRD analysis technique, the crystal structure of synthesized HCCDs was examined and the corresponding XRD pattern is shown in Fig. 4 . Owing to the polycrystalline nature of the HCCDs, sharp, narrow peaks were present in the diffraction pattern. The intense peak at 2θ value at 14.7 corresponds to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon. Whereas, the 2θ values at 31.2 corresponding to the (002) [26] plane of graphitic carbon (JCPDS Card Files, No. 41–1487) [27] indicates the formation of crystallized HCCD-dots [28]. The 2θ value at 38.2 corresponds to the (100) crystal plane [29].

The morphology and particle size of the prepared HCCDs were revealed using HR-TEM analysis ( Fig. 5 a & C). From HR-TEM analysis the particle size obtained is in between 2 to 5 nm and illustrates the spherical shape of the synthesized carbon dots. From the SAED pattern (Fig. 5b), it was depicted that the carbon dots are polycrystalline in nature which is in consistent with the XRD result. The XRD and SAED analysis in the case of HCCDs leads to an affirmation of the polycrystalline character of carbon dots (CDs). The XRD traces show crystalline peaks at 2θ = 31.2 and at 38.2, along with a broad peak between 2θ = 38 to 40, which can be assigned to amorphous carbon material. The dual nature of this indicates the coexistence of the crystalline and amorphous phases. This is further supported by the SAED result which showed broad diffuse rings forming a pattern indicative of poorly ordered polycrystallinity instead of sharp diffraction spots noted for very well-defined crystallinity. The absence of clear lattice fringe patterns in the HR-TEM images confirms the presence of small crystalline domains or overlapping amorphous regions with poorly resolvable and limited resolution. In summary, the outlined characteristics provide evidence for a polycrystalline structure with significant amorphous character, in concurrence with conclusions drawn by N. Sharma et al. (2022). Hence, a consistent explanation across the two diffraction methods leads to the conclusion that it is a polycrystalline material with limited order in its structure [29]. The HR-TEM image (Fig. 5d) of the HCCDs + capecitabine complex, which displays a disordered pattern as a result of complex formation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of experimental conditions for detection of capecitabine
ce:section.ce:para: To get the desired results, various experimental parameters need to be optimized. Therefore, the parameters such as pH response of HCCDs, concentration, absorbance of HCCDs, fluorescence intensity at various excitation wavelengths, and PL response over the reaction time were examined and illustrated in Fig. 6 . The excitation wavelength dependent PL spectra of HCCDs is shown in Fig. 6a, which suggest that excitation of HCCDs at 320 nm offers broad band PL spectrum peaking at 408 nm. It is attributed to the most probable transitions from π*→π. Whereas Fig. 6b depicts the absorption peak of HCCDs at 206 nm. CDs typically absorb between 200 and 300 nm. Furthermore, the quantum yield was determined to be 8.84 %. Fig. 6c shows the pH-dependent response of PL for HCCDs, suggesting that the basic pH 9 would be the best substitute for conducting the target analyte sensing experiment. The interaction of various functional groups present on the surface of HCCDs are found to be pH dependent, partial or complete deprotonation of these functional moieties are responsible for variation in intensities with pH. The absorbance spectrum of capecitabine shows a peak with wavelength maxima at 216 nm, which is because of the electronic transition of the singlet ground state (S0) to the first excited state (S1). Finally, the incubation time was investigated, and the maximum PL intensity was recorded at 10 min. Even 15 min later, the PL response does not change significantly (Fig. 6d). In addition, the temperature-dependent fluorescence (Fig. 6e) was recorded which depicts that at 30 ℃ (nearly equal to the room temperature) the reaction solution showed higher intensity and gradual decreases with the temperature up to 60 ℃, possibly due to the occurrence of thermally activated nonradiative relaxation [30]. Hence, 2 mL HCCDs, pH 9, 10 min incubation time at room temperature (30 ℃), and 320 nm excitation wavelength were preferred as an optimized experimental condition for the sensing experiment. Furthermore, the same optimized conditions provided reproducibility of results in triplicates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective fluorescent probing of capecitabine and interference study
ce:section.ce:para: In this particular experiment, the HCCDs concentration was fixed, and 10 µmolL−1 of Capecitabine, amino acids such as uracil, histidine, L- arginine, thymine, L-cystine, L-proline were introduced. Water was added to these solutions to bring its total volume up to 10 mL. The dilutions were homogenized by using a vortex mixture. The resultant solution's fluorescence intensities were measured at 320 nm excitation wavelength. To illustrate the selectivity of capecitabine, the PL intensities were recorded in the absence and presence of capecitabine as F0 and F, respectively, at a 408 nm emission wavelength.

The PL investigation study of HCCDs in the presence of several amino acids demonstrates that only capecitabine selectively enhances the fluorescence intensity of HCCDs, whilst other analytes had no apparent effect on the fluorescence intensity of HCCDs. As a consequence, it could be established that HCCDs have a selective binding ability towards capecitabine. Accordingly, other amino acids, instead of having the same uracil base, do not exhibit sensitivity towards HCCDs. This binding ability may be responsible for the selectivity of capecitabine. The change in fluorescence intensity (F/F0) of HCCDs with the addition of different amino acids at particular doses of 25 µmolL−1 is depicted in Fig. 7 a.

The specificity of HCCDs towards capecitabine was determined by measuring the change in PL intensities of HCCDs after adding capecitabine and other competing analytes at 320 nm excitation wavelength. The fluorescence intensity did not alter in the presence of different cations and anions (35 µmolL−1) (Fig. 7b and c). This suggests that HCCDs detect capecitabine preferentially and without interference, even in the presence of other analytes, demonstrating HCCDs selectivity for capecitabine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: HCCD as a sensor for capecitabine and validity of the method
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the analytical capability of HCCDs, fluorescence spectra were obtained under optimal conditions with various concentrations of capecitabine ranging from 0 to 55 µmolL−1 (Fig. 8 a & b). The results reveal that there was an increase in PL intensity in the linear range with increasing concentration of capecitabine. This can be ascribed to the complexation of the fluorophore seen in HCCDs with capecitabine. Furthermore, the ability of HCCDs was verified using a calibration curve as shown in Fig. 8c.

From the calibration curve, the obtained correlation coefficient value R2 was 0.992, which confirms the linear relationship between HCCDs and varied concentrations of capecitabine (0-55 µmolL−1). Moreover, the detection limit was calculated using Eq. (2) and is found to be 1.034 µmolL−1. The obtained LOD was considerably lower than the other reported LODs for capecitabine, and the comparison of the same is presented in Table 1 .
(2)
LOD =
3.3 σ
k
From the X and Y-axis, σ and k were obtained which represent standard deviations and slope of the calibration curve, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Insight in to the fluorescence enhancement mechanism and testing of real sample
ce:section.ce:para: The potential fluorescence enhancement mechanism is still an issue of debate. The possible mechanism in the current study can be explained by various investigations made during the research. The XPS data (Fig. 2c and d) indicates the presence of hydroxylic and carboxylic functional moieties on the surface of HCCDs. These functional groups offer opportunities to develop hydrogen bonding with capecitabine, which may be responsible for the PL enhancement. Consequently, due to the bonding of capecitabine with the surface of HCCDs, leads to the change in the surface defects, causes the fluorescence enhancement [31]. Thus, in this assay, capecitabine molecules operate as the acceptor of the CDs fluorescence emission. The HCCDs, upon excitation at 320 nm, transfer its excitation energy to the ground state molecule of capecitabine which results in the increased energy of the HCCD-Cap complex, causing the fluorescence enhancement. The PL intensity increases with an increase in the concentration of the capecitabine drug [32]. From the PL graph it was observed that the intensity is blue shifted from 408 nm to 398 nm with an increase in the concentration of capecitabine, proving the enhancement [33]. Additionally, to investigate the mechanism of PL enhancement of HCCDs, the HR- TEM and life time analysis techniques were carried out. The particle size of HCCDs obtained by TEM images is in the range of 2.84 to 4.36 nm (Fig. 5a, c), whereas the HCCDs-cap complex, the TEM image (Fig. 5d), shows that the size was increased and some disorder was found in the structure of HCCDs, which confirms the complexation within HCCDs and capecitabine. The lifetime measurement graph (Fig. 9 ) reveals that the lifetime of bare HCCDs and HCCDs-Cap complex are 7.17 and 7.85 respectively. The substantial increase in the lifetime in a composite of HCCDs and capecitabine proves the enhancement of fluorescence [34,35].

Furthermore, the composite formation is also verified by measuring zeta potential, this study shows the HCCDs and HCCDs-cap complex have −30.9 and −34.5 mV zeta potential respectively. The increase in zeta potential with the addition of capecitabine satisfies the condition for complex formation. The aforementioned conditions satisfy the emission enhancement mechanism. Fig. 9 illustrates the possible mechanism in the HCCDs.

In addition, to confirm the aforementioned mechanism, a pH study was performed. The pH value of bare HCCDs was 9, and pH value of the HCCDs + Capecitabine complex was observed to be 7.6. This decrease in the pH value of the mixture is due to the addition of capecitabine, suggesting the formation of an acidic environment in the mixture. Such an acidic environment was described by Yang et al. as the cause of the fluorescence enhancement, which was ascribed to the stronger hydrogen bond between the HCCDs and capecitabine [36].

The commercially available tablet containing capecitabine was chosen. The stock solution was prepared by dissolving the required amount of tablet in water by using a standard method. The different standard solutions were prepared by using stock solution and the concentration of solutions was maintained in the range applied in this study so as to get the desired results. The PL intensities of the prepared solutions were measured and by plotting the calibration graph the recovery of the sample is calculated and tabulated in Table 2 . The table shows the recovery of the sample for various concentrations. The data obtained are in agreement with the experimental expected value. Therefore, the proposed method could be applied for the quantitative determination of capecitabine from commercial samples by HCCDs as a fluorescence-based sensing probe with high selectivity, sensitivity and low detection limit.

The entire investigation in this work culminates in a unique, biocompatible, natural sensing probe produced by using honeycomb waste which is synthesized by utilizing an apparent, environment friendly, economical, and quick process. The as-synthesised HCCDs are polycrystalline by nature and do not require any additional experimental conditions beyond those required by several documented synthetic methodologies. The HCCDs have a 7.15 ns lifetime with brilliant green fluorescence. With a reduced detection limit of 1.04 µmolL−1, the HCCDs were effectively used as a sensing probe for the selective determination of capecitabine. It is discovered that the coefficient correlation value is 0.992, which is less than as compared to other documented analytical procedures. Excellent results from the selectivity research indicate that HCCDs sense capecitabine more selectively than other amino acids. The interference graph demonstrates that even in the presence of different analytes, HCCDs exhibit superior sensitivity towards only capecitabine. All-inclusive, it may be concluded that HCCDs serve as a sensitive, selective, and prominent sensing probe for the quantitative analysis of capecitabine drugs. Future research should focus on optimizing the quantum yield and surface functionalization of HCCDs to enhance their sensing capabilities and explore their potential in bioimaging or drug delivery applications by modifications. Further investigation into large-scale production methods and biocompatibility assessments will be crucial for developing a sustainable approach for pharmaceutical analysis and possible application.

Pradnya Khandagale: Investigation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Prathmesh Chougale: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Nafeesa Mujawar: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Muskan Momin: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Rohant Dhabbe: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Rajratna Kakade: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Sandip Nipane: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Sandip Sabale: Conceptualization, Supervision, Resources, Writing – review & editing. Deok-kee Kim: Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The author PK is thankful to BARTI Maharashtra for BANRF-2020 fellowship. We are thankful to the DST and DBT, New Delhi, India for the grant under the DST-FIST program (No/SR/FST/College-151/2013(C)) and for DBT-Star college scheme (HRD-11011/11/2022-HRD-DBT) respectively to Jaysingpur College, Jaysingpur.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A sensitive fluorescent sensor for selective determination of dichlorvos based on the recovered fluorescence of carbon dots-Cu(II) system
